---
title: Storage and preparation of defibrinated bovine blood for artificial tick feeding
author: Luís Adriano Anholeto
date: "2026-03-20"
version: 1.0
bibliography: references.bib
---

::: callout-important
# Safety Requirements

-   Wear **gloves, lab coat, and safety goggles** at all times.
-   Treat bovine blood as **potentially biohazardous material**.
-   Work inside a **biological safety cabinet (Class II)**.
-   Disinfect working surfaces before and after use using **70% ethanol**.
-   Dispose of blood waste according to **institutional biosafety regulations for biological materials**.
:::

# Purpose

This protocol describes the **standardized procedure for storing, handling, and preparing commercially sourced defibrinated bovine blood (Cedarlane)** for use in **artificial membrane feeding systems for ixodid ticks**.\
The protocol aims to maintain blood quality, minimize contamination and hemolysis, and ensure reproducible conditions during laboratory tick feeding experiments.

This protocol applies to laboratory experiments involving **artificial membrane feeding of hard ticks (Acari: Ixodidae)** using **commercially sourced defibrinated bovine blood**.

# Roles and Responsibilities

**Principal Investigator** - Ensures the protocol is implemented according to laboratory standards. - Approves revisions of the protocol.

**Laboratory Personnel** - Follow this SOP when handling and preparing blood for artificial tick feeding. - Maintain aseptic technique during aliquoting and preparation. - Record dates of blood opening, aliquoting, and expiration.

**Laboratory Manager** - Maintains supplies required for the protocol. - Ensures equipment are functioning properly.

# Definitions

**Defibrinated blood**\
Whole blood from which fibrin has been removed to prevent clot formation.

**Artificial membrane feeding**\
A laboratory method that allows ticks to feed on blood through an artificial membrane rather than on a live host.

**Aliquot**\
A measured portion of a larger blood sample stored separately to reduce contamination and repeated freeze–thaw or warming cycles.

# Materials

-   Cedarlane **defibrinated bovine blood**
-   Sterile D-glucose
-   ATP (adenosine triphosphate)
-   Gentamicin stock solution
-   Sterile 15 mL or 50 mL conical tubes for aliquots
-   Serological pipettes and sterile filtered tips
-   0.22 µm sterile syringe filters (for ATP solution if required)
-   70% ethanol for surface disinfection
-   Biosafety cabinet
-   Refrigerator set to **4 °C**
-   Water bath set to **37 °C**
-   Labels with date of receipt, date of first opening, and aliquot number

# Procedure

-   Prepare the 100 mg/mL glucose solution (click here for the protocol) in advance.

## Aliquoting protocol on the day the bottle is opened

1.  Gently invert the Cedarlane blood bottle several times to resuspend cells. Do **not** shake vigorously.
2.  Under the biological safety cabinet, dispense the blood into **single-use or single-day aliquots** (Falcon Tubes of 50mL).
3.  Add 1 mL of glucose stock solution at 100 mg/mL for each 50 mL of blood (final concentration of 2 g/L of glucose).
4.  Label each aliquot with:
    -   product
    -   lot number
    -   date opened
    -   aliquot volume
    -   planned expiration date
5.  Immediately return aliquots to **4 °C**.

## Immediately before feeding

For each aliquot needed for a blood change:

1.  Remove the aliquot from the refrigerator.
2.  Warm the blood to **37 °C** in a water bath.
3.  Add **5 µL of** **3 mM ATP per well** to the blood tube immediately before use [@khoo2022].
4.  Add **50 µL of 100x stock of penicillin/streptomycin/fungizone** per well to the blood tube immediately before use [@khoo2022].
5.  Mix gently by inversion.
6.  Transfer 4.5 mL of blood into a well of a fresh 6-well plate and follows the tick feeding protocol.

::: callout-important
# Shelf-life and storage conditions

## Temperature

Store blood at: - **4 °C** at all times when not in active use

## Maximum storage time

Use the following evidence-based limits:

-   **Best practice:** use within **3–5 days after first opening**
-   **Upper limit:** **7 days at 4 °C**, provided the blood remains visibly acceptable and has been handled aseptically

## Light protection

Store aliquots protected from light when possible.

## Acceptance criteria before use

Before each feeding, inspect the aliquot. Use only blood that: - remains uniformly fluid after gentle inversion - shows no obvious foul odour - has no visible clumps, fibrin strands, or heavy precipitate - has no obvious colour change suggestive of extensive hemolysis - has no turbidity suggestive of contamination Discard the aliquot if any of the above are present.
:::

## Suggested aliquot size

Use aliquots matched to one day of feeding. For example: - if one feeder requires 10 mL every 12 h, prepare aliquots in multiples of daily use - avoid large shared aliquots that must be repeatedly opened over several days

# Revision History

| Version | Date       | Author         | Description      |
|---------|------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1.0     | 2026-03-20 | L. A. Anholeto | Initial protocol |

<button onclick="window.print()" class="print-button" title="Print Protocol">

<i class="bi bi-printer"></i>

</button>
